exercises.title: gRPC vs REST vs GraphQL — choose the right API
exercises.instructions:
For each scenario, choose gRPC, REST, or GraphQL and explain why:
A: Zerodha order matching engine → risk engine (0.1ms latency required, internal only)
B: HDFC Bank public API (mobile app, browser, third-party integrators)
C: Flipkart product search (clients need different fields — some want name+price, some want full details)
D: Razorpay webhook notification service (simple, outbound HTTP)
E: Zerodha trade data streaming (real-time price updates every 100ms)
exercises.expected_solution:
A: Internal high-performance → gRPC
   0.1ms requirement: only gRPC achieves this (binary + HTTP/2)
   Internal only: no browser clients; gRPC works perfectly
   Strong typing: proto schema = clear contract between services

B: Public API → REST
   Browser/mobile: gRPC needs special proxy (grpc-web) for browsers
   Third-party integrators: REST is universal, everyone knows HTTP+JSON
   Human readable: debugging with curl/Postman is easy
   Public APIs: REST is the industry standard expectation

C: Variable field selection → GraphQL
   Different clients need different fields: GraphQL's core strength
   Under-fetching/over-fetching: REST returns all or nothing; GraphQL returns exactly what's asked
   Aggregated data: GraphQL can combine multiple resources in one query

D: Webhook outbound notification → REST (HTTP POST)
   Webhooks: just HTTP POST to a URL — REST is the universal choice
   No special protocol needed: any HTTP server can receive webhooks
   Retry on failure: standard HTTP retry mechanisms

E: Real-time streaming → gRPC (server streaming) OR WebSocket
   gRPC server streaming: `rpc WatchPrices(WatchRequest) returns (stream PriceUpdate)`
   100ms updates: gRPC streaming is efficient (keeps HTTP/2 connection open)
   Typed protocol: every price update is type-safe PriceUpdate proto message
   Alternative: WebSocket + JSON (more browser-friendly but slower)

test_cases.expected:
A: Internal high-performance → gRPC
   0.1ms requirement: only gRPC achieves this (binary + HTTP/2)
   Internal only: no browser clients; gRPC works perfectly
   Strong typing: proto schema = clear contract between services

B: Public API → REST
   Browser/mobile: gRPC needs special proxy (grpc-web) for browsers
   Third-party integrators: REST is universal, everyone knows HTTP+JSON
   Human readable: debugging with curl/Postman is easy
   Public APIs: REST is the industry standard expectation

C: Variable field selection → GraphQL
   Different clients need different fields: GraphQL's core strength
   Under-fetching/over-fetching: REST returns all or nothing; GraphQL returns exactly what's asked
   Aggregated data: GraphQL can combine multiple resources in one query

D: Webhook outbound notification → REST (HTTP POST)
   Webhooks: just HTTP POST to a URL — REST is the universal choice
   No special protocol needed: any HTTP server can receive webhooks
   Retry on failure: standard HTTP retry mechanisms

E: Real-time streaming → gRPC (server streaming) OR WebSocket
   gRPC server streaming: `rpc WatchPrices(WatchRequest) returns (stream PriceUpdate)`
   100ms updates: gRPC streaming is efficient (keeps HTTP/2 connection open)
   Typed protocol: every price update is type-safe PriceUpdate proto message
   Alternative: WebSocket + JSON (more browser-friendly but slower)

hints: Internal + latency → gRPC
Public/browser → REST
Variable fields → GraphQL
Streaming → gRPC server-streaming

exercises.title: Complete the proto file
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks to complete a valid Razorpay payment proto file.

blanks.hint: 64-bit integer for paise amounts (can exceed int32 max)
each field has a unique number

hints: syntax = 'proto3': required first line of every proto file
int64: for amounts in paise (can be large numbers)
rpc MethodName(Request) returns (Response): service method syntax

exercises.title: Write complete payment.proto file
exercises.instructions:
Write a complete proto file for Razorpay payment service:
1. PaymentRequest: merchantId, amountPaise, gateway, PaymentType enum
2. PaymentResponse: paymentId, status (PaymentStatus enum), errorMessage
3. PaymentStatus enum: INITIATED, PROCESSING, CAPTURED, FAILED, REFUNDED
4. PaymentService: CreatePayment + GetPaymentStatus RPCs
5. Verify field numbers are correct and unique
hints: java_multiple_files = true: one .java file per message (cleaner)
Enum first value = 0: proto3 requirement (use UNSPECIFIED)
Field numbers 1-15: 1-byte encoding — put common fields here

exercises.title: Protobuf field number rules and reserved
exercises.instructions:
Demonstrate proto field number rules:
1. Write AccountRequest with 3 fields
2. Show what happens when field removed: add 'reserved 2;'
3. Write backward-compatible extension: add new field with new number
4. Explain: why field numbers cannot be reused
5. @SpringBootTest: document the rules
hints: reserved NUMBER: prevents that field number from being reused
reserved 'name': prevents that field name from being reused
Unknown fields: old clients sending removed fields → new servers drop them safely

exercises.title: Use generated protobuf Java classes
exercises.instructions:
Write Java code using generated protobuf classes:
1. Build a PaymentRequest using builder pattern
2. Set all fields (merchantId, amountPaise, gateway, type)
3. Serialize to bytes + deserialize back
4. Assert fields match after round-trip
5. @SpringBootTest: builder + serialization round-trip
exercises.starter_code:
import com.google.protobuf.*;
// Assuming PaymentRequest, PaymentStatus are generated from payment.proto
hints: Protobuf builder: newBuilder() → set fields → build() (returns immutable)
toByteArray(): serialize to binary bytes (much smaller than JSON)
parseFrom(bytes): deserialize back — works across any language (Java, Python, Go)

exercises.title: Proto for Zerodha trade service
exercises.instructions:
Write trade.proto for Zerodha order management:
1. OrderRequest: symbol, quantity, price, OrderType enum (BUY/SELL/LIMIT/SL)
2. OrderResponse: orderId, status (PLACED/REJECTED/FILLED), fillPrice
3. MarketDataRequest: symbol, fromTimestamp, toTimestamp
4. TradeService: PlaceOrder (unary) + GetMarketData (server streaming)
5. Document: what 'stream' keyword in returns means
hints: returns (stream Type): server-side streaming RPC
stream keyword in request: client-side streaming
stream in both: bidirectional streaming

exercises.title: Protobuf pom.xml configuration
exercises.instructions:
Write the Maven configuration to compile proto files:
1. pom.xml dependency: grpc-stub, grpc-protobuf, protobuf-java
2. protobuf-maven-plugin: reads .proto → generates Java
3. os-maven-plugin: detects OS for correct protoc binary
4. Explain: what files are generated from payment.proto
exercises.expected_solution:
<!-- pom.xml gRPC and protobuf dependencies -->
<dependencies>
  <!-- gRPC runtime -->
  <dependency>
    <groupId>io.grpc</groupId>
    <artifactId>grpc-stub</artifactId>
    <version>1.62.2</version>
  </dependency>
  <dependency>
    <groupId>io.grpc</groupId>
    <artifactId>grpc-protobuf</artifactId>
    <version>1.62.2</version>
  </dependency>
  <!-- Protobuf Java runtime -->
  <dependency>
    <groupId>com.google.protobuf</groupId>
    <artifactId>protobuf-java</artifactId>
    <version>3.25.3</version>
  </dependency>
  <!-- Spring Boot gRPC server (net.devh) -->
  <dependency>
    <groupId>net.devh</groupId>
    <artifactId>grpc-server-spring-boot-starter</artifactId>
    <version>3.1.0.RELEASE</version>
  </dependency>
  <dependency>
    <groupId>net.devh</groupId>
    <artifactId>grpc-client-spring-boot-starter</artifactId>
    <version>3.1.0.RELEASE</version>
  </dependency>
</dependencies>

<build>
  <extensions>
    <!-- Detect OS for correct protoc binary (linux/mac/windows) -->
    <extension>
      <groupId>kr.motd.maven</groupId>
      <artifactId>os-maven-plugin</artifactId>
      <version>1.7.1</version>
    </extension>
  </extensions>
  <plugins>
    <plugin>
      <groupId>org.xolstice.maven.plugins</groupId>
      <artifactId>protobuf-maven-plugin</artifactId>
      <version>0.6.1</version>
      <configuration>
        <!-- .proto files location -->
        <protoSourceRoot>${project.basedir}/src/main/proto</protoSourceRoot>
        <!-- protoc binary -->
        <protocArtifact>com.google.protobuf:protoc:3.25.3:exe:${os.detected.classifier}</protocArtifact>
        <!-- gRPC Java plugin -->
        <pluginId>grpc-java</pluginId>
        <pluginArtifact>io.grpc:protoc-gen-grpc-java:1.62.2:exe:${os.detected.classifier}</pluginArtifact>
      </configuration>
      <executions>
        <execution>
          <goals>
            <goal>compile</goal>       <!-- generate message classes -->
            <goal>compile-custom</goal> <!-- generate gRPC service stubs -->
          </goals>
        </execution>
      </executions>
    </plugin>
  </plugins>
</build>

<!--
FROM payment.proto, protobuf-maven-plugin generates:
  PaymentRequest.java    ← message class (immutable, builder)
  PaymentResponse.java   ← message class
  PaymentType.java       ← enum class
  PaymentStatus.java     ← enum class
  PaymentServiceGrpc.java ← service stubs:
    - PaymentServiceImplBase: abstract class to implement on server
    - PaymentServiceBlockingStub: sync client
    - PaymentServiceFutureStub: async client (ListenableFuture)
    - PaymentServiceStub: async client (StreamObserver callback)
-->

public class GrpcPomDoc { public static void main(String[] a) {
    System.out.println("Proto generates: Message.java + ServiceGrpc.java (stubs + base)");
} }
hints: protobuf-maven-plugin: compile goal = messages; compile-custom = gRPC stubs
os-maven-plugin: selects correct protoc binary for your OS
grpc-server-spring-boot-starter (net.devh): the most popular Spring Boot gRPC library

exercises.title: Protobuf size vs JSON comparison
exercises.instructions:
Write a comparison showing protobuf binary size vs JSON:
1. Create a PaymentRequest (same data in both)
2. Measure JSON length in characters
3. Explain protobuf encoding advantages
4. Calculate size savings for 10M requests/day
5. @SpringBootTest: document the comparison
hints: JSON overhead: field names repeated for every message ("merchantId": 'MRC-001')
Proto: field numbers (1 byte) replace field names — massive savings at scale
10M × 60 bytes saved = 600MB/day in network traffic

exercises.title: Well-known types and wrapper types
exercises.instructions:
Demonstrate proto well-known types:
1. google.protobuf.Timestamp for createdAt field
2. google.protobuf.StringValue for optional nullable field
3. google.protobuf.Any for polymorphic payload
4. Import statements for well-known types
5. Explain: why StringValue over plain string (nullable semantics)
hints: Timestamp: google.protobuf.Timestamp — nanosecond precision datetime
StringValue: optional/nullable string (proto3 has no null for primitives)
repeated: becomes List<T> in Java; map<K,V>: becomes Map<K,V>

exercises.title: Protobuf encoding deep-dive
exercises.expected_solution:
1. Field 1 (merchant_id = 'MRC-001'):
   Tag: (field_number=1 << 3) | wire_type=2 (length-delimited) = 0x0A (1 byte)
   Length: 7 bytes for 'MRC-001' → varint 0x07 (1 byte)
   String data: 'MRC-001' → 7 ASCII bytes
   Total field 1: 1 + 1 + 7 = 9 bytes

2. Varint encoding for amount_paise=50000:
   50000 in binary: 1100001101010000
   Varint: splits into 7-bit groups with continuation bit:
   Group 1: 0x50 | 0x80 = 0xD0 (most significant 7 bits + continuation)
   Group 2: 0x86 | 0x80 = 0x86 (next 7 bits + continuation)
   Group 3: 0x03 (remaining bits, no continuation)
   Total: 3 bytes for 50000 (vs 8 bytes for int64 fixed)
   Field 2 total: tag(1B) + varint(3B) = 4 bytes

3. Size comparison:
   Proto total estimate: field1(9) + field2(4) + field3(1+1+4=6) = 19 bytes
   JSON equivalent: {"merchantId":"MRC-001","amountPaise":50000,"gateway":"HDFC"} = 66 bytes
   Ratio: 66/19 ≈ 3.5x smaller (typical: proto is 3-10x smaller than JSON)

4. Adding field 4 (currency) — backward compatibility:
   Old clients (before field 4 added): do NOT send field 4 data
   New server: receives old message, field 4 = default value (empty string)
   → Old clients WORK with new server (backward compatible)
   New clients: send field 4 = 'INR'
   Old server (before field 4 in schema): receives field 4, DROPS IT (unknown field)
   → New clients WORK with old server (forward compatible)
   Result: proto3 is backward AND forward compatible — just add fields!

test_cases.expected:
1. Field 1 (merchant_id = 'MRC-001'):
   Tag: (field_number=1 << 3) | wire_type=2 (length-delimited) = 0x0A (1 byte)
   Length: 7 bytes for 'MRC-001' → varint 0x07 (1 byte)
   String data: 'MRC-001' → 7 ASCII bytes
   Total field 1: 1 + 1 + 7 = 9 bytes

2. Varint encoding for amount_paise=50000:
   50000 in binary: 1100001101010000
   Varint: splits into 7-bit groups with continuation bit:
   Group 1: 0x50 | 0x80 = 0xD0 (most significant 7 bits + continuation)
   Group 2: 0x86 | 0x80 = 0x86 (next 7 bits + continuation)
   Group 3: 0x03 (remaining bits, no continuation)
   Total: 3 bytes for 50000 (vs 8 bytes for int64 fixed)
   Field 2 total: tag(1B) + varint(3B) = 4 bytes

3. Size comparison:
   Proto total estimate: field1(9) + field2(4) + field3(1+1+4=6) = 19 bytes
   JSON equivalent: {"merchantId":"MRC-001","amountPaise":50000,"gateway":"HDFC"} = 66 bytes
   Ratio: 66/19 ≈ 3.5x smaller (typical: proto is 3-10x smaller than JSON)

4. Adding field 4 (currency) — backward compatibility:
   Old clients (before field 4 added): do NOT send field 4 data
   New server: receives old message, field 4 = default value (empty string)
   → Old clients WORK with new server (backward compatible)
   New clients: send field 4 = 'INR'
   Old server (before field 4 in schema): receives field 4, DROPS IT (unknown field)
   → New clients WORK with old server (forward compatible)
   Result: proto3 is backward AND forward compatible — just add fields!

hints: Varint: variable-length integer (small numbers = fewer bytes)
Proto3: unknown fields are DROPPED (not errors) — enables forward compatibility
Tag = (field_number << 3) | wire_type: encodes both in 1 byte for fields 1-15

exercises.title: Complete Razorpay payment proto with all types
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Razorpay payment proto:
1. PaymentRequest: all scalar types + enum + repeated tags
2. PaymentResponse: Timestamp, optional failureReason, metadata map
3. PaymentService: CreatePayment + ListPayments (returns stream)
4. Backward-compatible: reserve removed fields
5. Java doc: what each type generates
hints: repeated → addTags() + getTagsList()
map → putMetadata() + getMetadataMap()
google.protobuf.Timestamp → seconds + nanos

exercises.title: Proto versioning strategy for Razorpay
exercises.instructions:
Design a proto versioning strategy:
1. PaymentRequestV1: 3 fields (merchantId, amount, gateway)
2. PaymentRequestV2: adds currency field (backward compatible)
3. PaymentRequestV3: removes gateway, adds gatewayConfig message
4. Show: which changes are safe, which are breaking
5. @SpringBootTest: document versioning rules
hints: Adding fields: always safe (old code ignores unknown fields)
Removing without reserved: DANGER — number can be reused accidentally
Package versioning: for major breaking changes (v1 → v2)

exercises.title: Complete Zerodha trading proto schema
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Zerodha trading proto:
1. OrderRequest (all 4 order types) + OrderResponse
2. MarketDataService: unary GetQuote + server-stream WatchQuotes
3. PortfolioService: GetPortfolio + streaming GetPositions
4. All enums with UNSPECIFIED=0
5. Java: demonstrate builder for complex nested message
hints: stream Position: GetPortfolio returns each position as a separate message
idempotency_key: prevents duplicate orders on retry
Multiple services per proto: allowed — separate by domain (Trade, Market, Portfolio)

exercises.title: Protobuf error handling patterns
exercises.instructions:
Design error handling for gRPC services:
1. Use google.rpc.Status for rich error details
2. ErrorDetail message: code, message, fieldViolations
3. gRPC status codes: NOT_FOUND, INVALID_ARGUMENT, UNAVAILABLE
4. Map proto errors to gRPC status in service implementation
5. @SpringBootTest: document error patterns
hints: StatusRuntimeException: standard gRPC exception class
Status.UNAVAILABLE: retryable (gateway down), Status.INVALID_ARGUMENT: not retryable
Status.withDescription(): adds human-readable error message

exercises.title: Complete M28 T1 gRPC Basics synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build complete M28 T1 synthesis:
1. payment.proto: PaymentRequest + PaymentResponse + enums
2. PaymentRequest builder in Java (simulated)
3. Serialization round-trip
4. Version evolution: add new field (safe)
5. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): build, serialize, version-safe add field, reserved check
exercises.starter_code: // Complete M28 T1 gRPC Basics synthesis
hints: Builder pattern: set → build (immutable result)
Round-trip: serialize → deserialize → same values
V2 backward compat: empty string default for missing currency field
